profile.country_or_body: United States — Palantir federal government footprint (ICE, DoD/Army, IRS/HHS/CDC, intelligence community)
profile.summary: The US is Palantir's home turf and largest single government market: roughly $1.88B of its $4.5B FY2025 revenue came from the government segment, ~74% of total revenue from US customers (Palantir FY2025 10-K). Palantir is deeply operational across federal agencies: ICE deportation tooling (ImmigrationOS, $30M, 2025), DoD/Army (Maven Smart System ceiling raised to ~$1.3B; TITAN $178.4M; Army Vantage ~$458M; a 2025 Army enterprise agreement worth up to $10B), CDC/HHS public-health data (HHS Protect, Tiberius, a $443M CDC consolidated disease-surveillance contract), and an IRS-led federal data-consolidation ("mega-database") effort following Trump's March 20, 2025 executive order to eliminate inter-agency information silos. By contrast, Splink (the UK MoJ's open-source probabilistic record-linkage library) has only a marginal, mostly negative US footprint: the Census Bureau evaluated Splink against its in-house BigMatch and chose BigMatch (faster/more accurate at scale); the NIH All of Us Research Program uses Datavant's privacy-preserving record linkage, not Splink. The honest characterization is DIFFERENT TOOLS, SAME COUNTRY: Palantir (proprietary Foundry/Gotham platform) and Splink (an open-source statistical linkage library) occupy the same broad "joining-up government data" trajectory but are not the same body, dataset, or tool, and Palantir is vastly more operational. Palantir does NOT use Splink. Tiered THEMATIC_ADJACENCY: same national trajectory, no shared body/dataset, and Splink's US use is pilot/evaluation/negative.
profile.palantir_product: Foundry; Gotham; Maven Smart System; Army Vantage; TITAN ground stations; AIP
palantir_presence: ICE / DHS — ImmigrationOS ('Immigration Lifecycle Operating System'): ~$30M modification to an existing ICE contract, awarded ~April 2025, prototype due Sept 25, 2025, contract running through Sept 2027. Cited as supporting Executive Orders 14159 and 13773. Purpose: near-real-time visibility into the 'immigration lifecycle' — identify/apprehend removal-priority individuals, track self-deportations, and streamline deportation logistics.

ICE — reporting (EFF, Jan 2026) that ICE is using a Palantir tool fed by Medicaid data to identify immigrants; EFF asked a federal judge to block use of Medicaid data for deportation and filed an amicus brief in litigation over ICE's grab for IRS taxpayer data.
USCIS — Dec 2025 reporting (Fortune) of a new Palantir contract for a tech platform at another ICE-adjacent federal agency.
US Army / DoD — Maven Smart System: ceiling raised to ~$1.3B through 2029 (up from initial ~$480M); demand-driven increase reported May 2025. Battlefield AI/targeting and sensor-data fusion.
US Army — TITAN (Tactical Intelligence Targeting Access Node): $178.4M OTA awarded March 2024 for 10 AI-enabled ground-station prototypes (next-gen targeting/intelligence).
US Army — Army Vantage: ~$458M four-year agreement, unified data management across personnel, logistics, combat readiness.
US Army — 2025 enterprise software agreement reported worth up to $10B over 10 years, consolidating numerous prior contracts.

CDC — five-year, $443M consolidated disease-surveillance / 'common operating picture' contract (announced ~late 2022) leveraging Palantir for outbreak planning/response; ties together HHS Protect, ASPR Engage, Tiberius, and the DCIPHER program.

HHS — Tiberius: built mid-2020 under Operation Warp Speed for vaccine allocation; contract renewed/expanded to ~$31M. HHS Protect: ~$24.9M no-bid contract (April 2020) building the COVID-19 data system.

IRS / federal data consolidation — Palantir engineers embedded at the IRS reportedly building a 'mega API'/single searchable hub unifying IRS data, expanding from Trump's March 20, 2025 EO on inter-agency data sharing; talks reported with SSA and Dept. of Education on adopting Foundry; existing work with HHS and the Pentagon (NYT reporting; Intercept, April 2026, describing 'massive-scale' IRS data mining). Palantir denies it can 'proactively share data across federal government sources' (May 30, 2025 blog post).

Intelligence community — long history with CIA (In-Q-Tel early investor) and work with FBI/NSA-adjacent bodies; relationship reported as more limited/rocky than Palantir's public reputation implies (BuzzFeed 2017).
profile.contract_values_dates: ICE ImmigrationOS: ~$30M, awarded ~April 2025, prototype due Sept 25 2025, runs to Sept 2027. Maven Smart System: ceiling ~$1.3B through 2029 (from ~$480M), increase reported May 23 2025. TITAN: $178.4M OTA, March 6 2024. Army Vantage: ~$458M, four-year. Army enterprise agreement: up to $10B / 10 years, reported Aug 2025. CDC: $443M / five years (announced ~Nov 2022). HHS Tiberius: renewed/expanded to ~$31M. HHS Protect: ~$24.9M no-bid, April 2020. IRS mega-database: contract values not fully public; congressional letters June 17 2025; EO signed March 20 2025. profile.operational_use: Palantir's Foundry/Gotham/AIP platforms ingest, integrate, and make searchable large volumes of citizen and operational data across agencies. Operational uses: ICE uses ImmigrationOS to locate, prioritize, and track immigrants for removal and to monitor self-deportations (reportedly drawing on cross-agency data including, per EFF, Medicaid records); DoD/Army uses Maven and TITAN for AI-assisted targeting and battlefield/intelligence sensor fusion and Vantage for readiness/logistics data; CDC/HHS uses Palantir for disease surveillance, vaccine allocation (Tiberius) and pandemic data (HHS Protect); and the IRS/federal effort aims to build a unified, searchable cross-agency data hub (tax, immigration, Social Security records) — the core of the 'mega-database' concern.
datasets: IRS taxpayer records (reported core of the 'mega-database')
Social Security Administration data (reported Foundry-adoption talks)
Dept. of Education data (reported talks)
Medicaid / HHS health data (per EFF reporting, fed into an ICE-used Palantir tool)
Immigration / ICE enforcement and visa-overstay data (ImmigrationOS)
CDC disease-surveillance, HHS Protect COVID-19, and Tiberius vaccine-allocation data
DoD/Army operational, intelligence, sensor, personnel, logistics and targeting data (Maven, TITAN, Vantage)
profile.splink_side: Per the prior global Splink analysis and primary sources, the US use of Splink is pilot/evaluation/negative: (1) the US Census Bureau evaluated Splink against its in-house BigMatch on speed, accuracy and work requirements and chose BigMatch (Census research outputs); (2) the NIH All of Us Research Program does NOT use Splink — its record linkage is done via Datavant's privacy-preserving record linkage (PPRL) through the Center for Linkage and Acquisition of Data (CLAD); (3) the gov.uk Splink page cites the US Census Bureau only in historical Fellegi-Sunter context, not as a Splink user. So in the US, Splink is largely absent operationally while Palantir is dominant. Palantir relates to the Splink story only as the proprietary, far-more-operational instance of the same broad trajectory (consolidating/joining-up government data on citizens). Different tools, different (or no overlapping) bodies, same country. No evidence Palantir uses Splink, and no documented case of Palantir and Splink in the same agency/dataset.
adjudications: Germany, Federal Constitutional Court (Bundesverfassungsgericht), Judgment of 16 February 2023 (1 BvR 1547/19, 1 BvR 2634/20): the automated data-analysis powers in Hesse's Security and Public Order Act (under which police ran Palantir's Gotham as 'hessenDATA') and Hamburg's police data law were ruled UNCONSTITUTIONAL for violating the right to informational self-determination (insufficient interference thresholds); such tools permissible only to protect particularly weighty legal interests against a sufficiently identifiable danger. NOTE: This is a German, not US, adjudication — relevant as the leading court ruling against a Palantir deployment internationally; legal challenges (e.g., GFF in Bavaria/NRW) continued afterward.

United States: EPIC v. ICE — EPIC settled a FOIA lawsuit against ICE concerning Palantir's role in mass surveillance/profiling and recovered attorneys' fees (settled ~Jan 2020). This is FOIA/transparency litigation, not a substantive court ruling on the legality of Palantir's data use.

United States (2025–2026): EFF litigation/amicus activity challenging cross-agency data consolidation (sued over DOGE access to OPM data; amicus in suit over ICE's grab for IRS taxpayer data; suit vs State/DHS on a mass-surveillance program) — ongoing, no final adjudication squarely against Palantir located.
No US court or regulator final ruling specifically holding Palantir's federal data use unlawful was found as of the research date.
controversies: IRS/federal 'mega-database': June 17, 2025 letter from Sen. Wyden, Rep. Ocasio-Cortez and other Democrats accusing Palantir of enabling/profiting from violations of federal law (Privacy Act notice/transparency requirements, tax-privacy laws) by consolidating Americans' data; Palantir denies it can proactively share data across agencies (May 30, 2025 blog).

ICE ImmigrationOS: civil-liberties and immigrant-rights groups (American Immigration Council, EPIC, EFF, State of Surveillance) warn it industrializes deportation targeting and self-deportation tracking using cross-agency citizen data.
EFF (Jan 2026) reporting that an ICE Palantir tool feeds on Medicaid data to identify immigrants.
Privacy-law concerns that building a single searchable cross-agency hub (tax + immigration + SSA) likely breaches the Privacy Act and IRC tax-confidentiality provisions.
Germany BVerfG Feb 2023 ruling against Gotham/hessenDATA (international flagship controversy over Palantir policing software).
Historical: Palantir's cultivated-but-overstated intelligence-community reputation (BuzzFeed 2017).

unverified_flags: Exact dollar value of Palantir's IRS/federal data-consolidation work is not fully public; the 'mega-database' characterization comes from NYT/Intercept reporting and congressional letters, and Palantir disputes some claims.
The $10B Army enterprise-agreement figure is an 'up to / ceiling' value over 10 years reported in trade press, not necessarily obligated spend.
EFF's report of ICE using a Palantir tool fed by Medicaid data is reporting/allegation, subject to ongoing litigation, not an adjudicated finding.
Census Bureau 'chose BigMatch over Splink' is drawn from Census research summaries surfaced in search; the precise published evaluation document and date were not individually pinned in this pass.
The specific 'Indiana pilot' of Splink referenced in the lead brief was not independently located in primary sources during this research.
Intelligence-community (CIA/NSA/FBI) Palantir relationship is partly historical and reputationally inflated per 2017 reporting; current scope not fully verified.
FDA-specific Palantir contract not confirmed in this pass (lead mentioned FDA; not independently verified here).
profile.caveats: Palantir does NOT use Splink, and no documented instance places Palantir and Splink in the same US agency or dataset. The connection is THEMATIC_ADJACENCY only: both belong to the broad 'joining-up government data' trajectory, but in the US Splink is essentially non-operational (Census evaluated and chose BigMatch; All of Us uses Datavant) while Palantir is the dominant, heavily operational data-integration vendor. The flagship court adjudication against a Palantir deployment (Germany's BVerfG, 16 Feb 2023) is German, not US — included for completeness, not as a US ruling. US litigation (EPIC, EFF) is FOIA/ongoing and has not produced a final ruling holding Palantir's federal data use unlawful. Contract values for the IRS/data-consolidation effort are partly unconfirmed and disputed by Palantir.

overclaims: INTERNAL MATH CONTRADICTION: The summary states '$1.88B of its $4.5B FY2025 revenue came from the government segment,' but the contract_values_dates field says government segment = '54% of $4.5B.' 54% of $4.5B is $2.43B, not $1.88B. The two figures are mutually inconsistent and at least one is fabricated. The 10-K confirms 54% government / 74% US-customer, so the dollar figure '$1.88B' has no source and must be removed or corrected to ~$2.43B.

TIER OVERSTATED IN THE WRONG DIRECTION — but note this is a downgrade-resistance point, not an upgrade: as a hostile critic I flag that 'THEMATIC_ADJACENCY' is actually generous to the linkage thesis because it implies a meaningful thematic tie. The profile's own evidence shows Splink is non-operational in the US (Census rejected it for BigMatch; All of Us uses Datavant, not Splink) and Palantir does not use Splink. The honest tier is SAME_DOMAIN_SAME_COUNTRY at most: the ONLY shared attribute is 'US government' + the vague abstraction 'joining-up data.' There is zero shared body, dataset, tool, contract, or document. The 'same broad trajectory' framing is editorializing, not a documented connection.

The phrase 'Palantir... occupy the same broad joining-up government data trajectory' is an analyst's narrative construct, not a sourced fact. No primary source links the two; it should be explicitly labeled as the author's framing, not presented as characterization of record.

USCIS Dec 2025 'new Palantir contract' (Fortune) is described in palantir_presence as fact ('a new Palantir contract for a tech platform at another ICE-adjacent federal agency') but the cited Fortune headline frames it as a platform for 'another federal agency that works with ICE' — the specific scope/value is single-source trade reporting and should carry an unverified flag, which it currently lacks.

'Indiana pilot' of Splink and 'FDA-specific Palantir contract' are referenced in unverified_flags as NOT located — correct to flag, but the lead brief that introduced them should not be relied upon; these must stay out of any asserted findings.

Army '$10B over 10 years' is a ceiling/'up to' figure from trade press (AIN), not obligated spend — the profile does flag this, but the summary and operational_use sections repeat 'worth up to $10B' without the obligated-vs-ceiling caveat inline.
strongest_sourced_claims: ICE ImmigrationOS ~$30M modification, awarded ~April 2025, prototype due Sept 25 2025, running through Sept 2027 — corroborated by Axios, American Immigration Council, State of Surveillance, and ICE policy trackers.
Maven Smart System ceiling raised to ~$1.3B through 2029 (from ~$480M), reported May 23 2025 — confirmed by DefenseScoop, SpaceNews, InsideDefense; the $795M increment is consistent across sources.
TITAN: $178.4M OTA (Phase 3), awarded March 6 2024, 10 ground-station prototypes (5 advanced / 5 basic) — confirmed by US Army (army.mil), DefenseScoop, C4ISRNET, Breaking Defense.

CDC $443M five-year consolidated disease-surveillance 'Common Operating Picture' contract, announced Dec 2022, uniting HHS Protect / ASPR Engage / Tiberius / DCIPHER — confirmed by FedScoop, CDC/Palantir PRNewswire release, MeriTalk.

Bundesverfassungsgericht Judgment of 16 Feb 2023 (1 BvR 1547/19, 1 BvR 2634/20) ruling Hesse (HSOG 25a) and Hamburg (HmbPolDVG 49) automated-data-analysis provisions unconstitutional re informational self-determination; Gotham ran as 'hessenDATA' — confirmed by BVerfG press release and judgment. Correctly flagged as GERMAN, not US.
FY2025 10-K segmentation: 54% government / 46% commercial, 74% US revenue, on $4.5B total — confirmed against the SEC 10-K and multiple summaries.

Splink-side negative US footprint: Census Bureau evaluated Splink vs in-house BigMatch and favored BigMatch on speed/accuracy (Census CSRM materials); NIH All of Us uses Datavant PPRL via CLAD, NOT Splink (Datavant press release, NIH) — both confirmed.

EPIC v. ICE (Palantir/FALCON databases) FOIA suit settled ~Jan 31 2020 with attorneys' fees recovered — confirmed via EPIC; correctly characterized as FOIA/transparency, not a substantive ruling on legality.
unsupported_to_remove: The unsourced dollar figure '$1.88B' for FY2025 government-segment revenue — remove or correct to the 10-K-consistent ~$2.43B (54% of $4.5B); as written it contradicts the profile's own 54% statement.

Any implication that Splink and Palantir share a technical, methodological, or operational connection — there is NONE documented. The profile mostly handles this correctly (explicit 'Palantir does NOT use Splink'), but the recurring 'same broad trajectory' editorial framing should be demoted from summary/caveats to a clearly-labeled author inference, not a finding.
The 'Indiana pilot' of Splink — not located in primary sources; must not appear as an asserted fact anywhere (currently only in unverified_flags, which is acceptable, but it must never be promoted).
FDA-specific Palantir contract — not confirmed; keep out of palantir_presence and findings.
corrections: Replace '$1.88B of its $4.5B FY2025 revenue came from the government segment' with '~54% ($~2.43B) of $4.5B FY2025 revenue from the government segment (Palantir FY2025 10-K)' to remove the internal contradiction.

Downgrade connection_tier from THEMATIC_ADJACENCY to SAME_DOMAIN_SAME_COUNTRY: the only documented common factor is 'US federal data-integration domain.' No shared body, dataset, tool, or document exists, and Splink is non-operational in the US per the profile's own evidence. 'Thematic adjacency' overstates the strength of the tie.
Label the 'same broad joining-up government data trajectory' language explicitly as analyst framing/inference, not as a documented characterization.
Add an unverified flag to the USCIS Dec 2025 Fortune contract item (single-source trade reporting; scope/value not independently confirmed).
Keep the inline obligated-vs-ceiling caveat next to every mention of the '$10B' Army figure, including in summary and operational_use, not only in unverified_flags.
verification.notes: Adversarial verdict: The Palantir-side factual scaffolding is SOLID — ImmigrationOS $30M, Maven $1.3B, TITAN $178.4M, CDC $443M, the BVerfG 16 Feb 2023 ruling, EPIC settlement, and the 10-K 54%/74% segmentation all check out against primary or strong secondary sources. The profile is also commendably honest that Palantir does NOT use Splink and that no US court has ruled Palantir's federal data use unlawful. The defects are: (1) a hard internal math contradiction ($1.88B vs 54% of $4.5B = $2.43B) that must be fixed — the dollar figure is unsupported; (2) the connection_tier is overstated and should drop to SAME_DOMAIN_SAME_COUNTRY because the only real linkage is 'US government data-integration domain,' with Splink essentially absent operationally (Census chose BigMatch; All of Us uses Datavant) and zero shared body/dataset/tool; (3) the 'same trajectory' narrative is editorializing and must be marked as inference. As Palantir's lawyer I additionally note the profile already correctly hedges the IRS 'mega-database' (reporting/congressional-letter-sourced, Palantir disputes, no contract value, no adjudication) and the EFF Medicaid-data allegation (allegation, in active litigation, not adjudicated) — those hedges should remain firmly in place. No Splink<->Palantir technical link is asserted as fact, which is correct; the only required action is to demote the thematic 'trajectory' framing from characterization to labeled inference. Net: MIXED — strong sourcing, but the revenue-figure contradiction and an overstated tier require correction before this profile is defensible.

title: Germany — Palantir Gotham policing + the Constitutional Court ruling

profile.country_or_body: Germany — Palantir Gotham in state police (Hesse "hessenDATA", NRW "DAR", Bavaria "VeRA", Baden-Württemberg) and the Federal Constitutional Court (Bundesverfassungsgericht); Splink side: Federal Statistical Office (Destatis) register census
profile.summary: Two distinct data-linkage layers exist in the same country with no documented operational overlap. PALANTIR LAYER (policing/security): Palantir's Gotham platform powers automated cross-database police data-analysis systems in multiple German states — Hesse "hessenDATA" (since 2017), North Rhine-Westphalia "DAR" (since ~2019/20), Bavaria "VeRA" (live 2024), and Baden-Württemberg (contract signed March 2025, deployment from Q2 2026). On 16 February 2023 the Federal Constitutional Court (1 BvR 1547/19 and 1 BvR 2634/20, First Senate) struck down the enabling provisions in Hesse (§25a(1) 1st alt. HSOG) and Hamburg (§49(1) 1st alt. HmbPolDVG) as unconstitutional for violating the right to informational self-determination (Art. 2(1) in conjunction with Art. 1(1) Basic Law). Palantir/Gotham/hessenDATA is explicitly named in the judgment. This is a genuine Tier-1 court ruling touching Palantir-enabled policing. SPLINK LAYER (statistics): entirely separate — Germany's Federal Statistical Office (Destatis) self-reports on Splink's official MoJ page that it "uses Splink to conduct projects in linking register-based census data" for the German register census. CONNECTION: Same country, but different bodies (state police/interior ministries vs. federal statistics office), different data domains (criminal/security intelligence vs. census/register statistics), and different tools (Palantir Gotham vs. open-source Splink). There is NO evidence Palantir uses Splink, and NO evidence the police systems and Destatis census linkage share data, datasets, or infrastructure. The thread is thematic: both reflect German state "joining-up" of citizen records via automated linkage, but only the Palantir/policing layer has triggered a constitutional adjudication. Classified SAME_DOMAIN_SAME_COUNTRY in the loose sense (same country, automated record-linkage trajectory) but bordering THEMATIC_ADJACENCY because the bodies and data domains differ entirely.
palantir_presence: Hesse 'hessenDATA': Palantir Gotham acquired 2017 by direct award citing 'special urgency', later made permanent. Links previously unconnected police databases (records, telecoms metadata, social media) to surface time-critical cross-database connections about persons/places/events. Contract value deliberately concealed — purchase order reportedly listed a nominal 0.01 EUR with the true price kept secret citing Hesse's 'security interests'.
North Rhine-Westphalia 'DAR' (Datenbankübergreifende Analyse und Recherche): Palantir Gotham deployed since ~2019/20; GFF has filed a pending constitutional/legal challenge.

Bavaria 'VeRA' (Verfahrensübergreifende Recherche und Analyse): Palantir Gotham, operational 2024; GFF (with Chaos Computer Club support) filed a constitutional complaint in July 2024/2025 against the Bavarian police law's automated data-analysis powers.

Baden-Württemberg: state parliament approved a police-law revision; Gotham contract signed March 2025 for ~25 million EUR (price-locked; without the price-binding clause cost would have ~doubled to ~50M EUR; reportedly no exit clause), deployment from Q2 2026.
2025 reporting (heise) indicates German investigators also use the Palantir big-data analysis for minor crimes, broadening the scope beyond serious/terrorism cases.
profile.splink_side: Splink's official MoJ-hosted page lists Germany's Federal Statistical Office (Destatis) as a user: 'The German Federal Statistical Office (Destatis) uses Splink to conduct projects in linking register-based census data.' This is the German entry the prior global Splink analysis would have captured (self-reported on Splink's page). It sits in the federal statistics / census domain. Palantir relates to it only as 'same country, different layer': different body (Destatis, a federal statistics authority, vs. state police forces and interior ministries), different data domain (anonymised/administrative census register linkage vs. operational police intelligence), and different tool (open-source Splink vs. proprietary Palantir Gotham). No documented data-sharing, shared dataset, or shared infrastructure between the two. Palantir does NOT use Splink.

adjudications: Bundesverfassungsgericht (Federal Constitutional Court), First Senate, Judgment of 16 February 2023, 1 BvR 1547/19 (Hesse) and 1 BvR 2634/20 (Hamburg): held §25a(1) 1st alt. HSOG (Hesse) and §49(1) 1st alt. HmbPolDVG (Hamburg) — the automated police data-analysis/data-evaluation powers — UNCONSTITUTIONAL for breaching the right to informational self-determination (Art. 2(1) in conjunction with Art. 1(1) Basic Law) due to insufficient intervention thresholds. Palantir/Gotham/hessenDATA is explicitly named. Hamburg provision declared void immediately; Hesse provision allowed to continue under restrictive conditions until 30 September 2023 (or until new legislation) to give the legislature time to revise it. This is a genuine Tier-1 ruling directly touching Palantir-enabled policing.

Follow-on constitutional complaints (Gesellschaft für Freiheitsrechte / GFF): pending challenge to Bavaria's VeRA-enabling police law (filed 2024/2025, with Chaos Computer Club support); GFF also pursuing challenges relating to NRW and revised Hesse provisions. Outcomes not yet decided as of June 2026.
Splink side: NONE FOUND — no court or regulator ruling touching Destatis's Splink use or the register census linkage.
profile.palantir_product: Palantir Gotham (deployed under state-specific names: hessenDATA in Hesse, DAR in North Rhine-Westphalia, VeRA in Bavaria; unnamed/Gotham in Baden-Württemberg)
profile.contract_values_dates: Hesse hessenDATA: direct award 2017 (urgency procedure), later permanent; true price secret (purchase order reportedly nominal 0.01 EUR, real value withheld on 'security interests' grounds). NRW DAR: ~2019/20, value not publicly confirmed. Bavaria VeRA: operational 2024, value not publicly confirmed in sources reviewed. Baden-Württemberg: contract signed March 2025, ~25 million EUR (price-locked; would have ~doubled to ~50M EUR without price-binding clause; reportedly no exit clause), deployment from Q2 2026.
profile.operational_use: Police use Gotham to fuse and cross-reference previously siloed databases (police records, telecommunications/metadata, possibly social media and other administrative sources) to rapidly surface relationships between persons, places, and events for preventive crime-fighting and investigations. The BVerfG objected that the broad statutory powers permitted dragnet 'analysis by association' sweeping in unsuspected third parties without adequate intervention thresholds. 2025 reporting indicates use has extended to minor crimes, not just serious/terrorism cases.
datasets: Hesse: linked police operational databases plus external/telecoms data via hessenDATA (specific dataset inventory not fully public)
Destatis register census: administrative and statistical registers linked via Splink (entirely separate from police data; no documented overlap with Palantir systems)
controversies: Secrecy of the Hesse contract value (nominal 0.01 EUR on paper; real price withheld citing state security interests) — transparency/procurement concerns.
Direct, no-tender award to a US firm (Palantir) on 'special urgency' grounds, raising sovereignty and dependency concerns about a US-controlled platform handling German citizen/police data.
Reported expansion of Palantir analysis to minor crimes, widening surveillance scope.

Civil-society opposition (GFF, Chaos Computer Club) and continued state adoption (Baden-Württemberg 2025) despite the 2023 ruling — 'Palantir is hungry, Germany is on the menu' framing in critical press.
Baden-Württemberg contract reportedly has no exit clause and a price-binding clause, raising long-term lock-in concerns.
unverified_flags: Exact contract values for NRW (DAR) and Bavaria (VeRA) not confirmed in sources reviewed.
The '0.01 EUR' nominal Hesse purchase-order figure is from secondary reporting; the true contract value remains officially undisclosed.
Precise scope of datasets ingested by each Palantir deployment is not fully public.
Status/outcome of the pending GFF constitutional complaints (Bavaria, NRW, revised Hesse) not yet adjudicated as of June 2026.

Destatis's Splink use is self-reported on Splink's page; the precise scale, which specific register-census projects, and dates were not independently cross-verified from a Destatis primary publication in this pass.
profile.caveats: No documented link connects the Palantir policing layer and the Destatis/Splink census layer — different bodies, data domains, and tools. The connection is one of national context and the broad 'automated record-linkage of citizens' trajectory, not shared systems or data. I make no claim that Palantir uses Splink (it does not). The 2023 BVerfG ruling is the load-bearing, well-verified Tier-1 fact and is squarely on the Palantir side. profile.country_or_body: Australia (federal) — Palantir in Defence / AUSTRAC / ACIC / ASD vs. ABS Person Linkage Spine (PLIDA/MADIP) using Splink
profile.summary: Palantir has a substantial, documented Australian government footprint — but it sits in the national-security / law-enforcement / defence data domains, NOT in the statistical data-integration domain where Splink is used. Australia's Splink deployment is at the Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS), which used Splink for the first time in 2025 to build the Person Linkage Spine ("the Spine"), the linkage backbone of the Person Level Integrated Data Asset (PLIDA, formerly MADIP). The Spine is built from Medicare (Services Australia), DOMINO Centrelink data (Department of Social Services) and Personal Income Tax (ATO) records, and is used by approved researchers for statistics/policy. Palantir, by contrast, holds contracts with the Department of Defence (Foundry data integration/analytics, ~$26m+ cumulative since 2013; latest $7.6m Feb 2026 limited-tender for the Cyber & Electronic Warfare Division), AUSTRAC (data analytics, $8.1m 2023 grown past $12m via variations) and the ACIC (criminal-intelligence work; a Palantir Gotham-style system documented sorting ~42 million data points on Australians, ~$5.7m). Total federal spend reported at ~$50-60m, plus a ~$160m+ Future Fund equity stake. There is NO public evidence that Palantir contracts with the ABS, ATO, DSS or Services Australia for statistical integration, and NO evidence Palantir touches the Spine/PLIDA or uses Splink. The connection is therefore thematic — both are "joining up" siloed citizen data — but they occupy different bodies, different data domains, and different governance/legal regimes. Tier: THEMATIC_ADJACENCY (NOT same-domain-same-body, because Palantir's bodies and data domains differ from the ABS statistical-linkage environment).
profile.palantir_product: Foundry (and AIP) for Defence data integration/analytics; Gotham (or Gotham-style intelligence platform) for ACIC criminal-intelligence work; data-analytics platform for AUSTRAC financial intelligence. In Nov 2025 Palantir Foundry + AIP were assessed to PROTECTED level under the Australian IRAP scheme, broadening eligible government use.
palantir_presence: Department of Defence — Foundry data integration/analytics; cumulative ~$26m+ since 2013; $7.6m one-year limited-tender contract via AusTender 11 Feb 2026 for Cyber & Electronic Warfare Division; reporting of embedded Palantir staff and a 2024 contract ~$7.1m
AUSTRAC — $8.1m data-analytics contract (2023) expanded past $12m via five variations within ~12 months
ACIC — criminal-intelligence analytics; leaked Palantir manual documents system holding ~42m data points on Australians (intercepts, police/government databases, location data); ~$5.7m committed
Australian Signals Directorate (ASD) — listed among agencies with Palantir contracts
Victorian Department of Justice — named as Palantir customer (state level)
Future Fund — Palantir equity stake reported >$160m
Nov 2025 — Palantir Foundry + AIP assessed to IRAP PROTECTED level
profile.splink_side: Australia's Splink use is at the ABS: Splink (the UK MoJ open-source probabilistic record-linkage tool) was used for the first time in 2025 to construct the Person Linkage Spine, the core linkage infrastructure of PLIDA (formerly MADIP). Splink was also used for the 2024 National Linkage Spine underpinning the National Disability Data Asset. The Spine integrates Medicare Consumer Directory (Services Australia), DOMINO Centrelink Administrative Data (Department of Social Services) and Personal Income Tax (ATO); it is operational (covering Jan 2006–Jun 2025, updated at least annually) and serves approved-researcher statistical/policy projects. Palantir relates to this only thematically: it operates in DIFFERENT bodies (Defence, AUSTRAC, ACIC, ASD — not ABS/ATO/DSS/Services Australia), a DIFFERENT data domain (national security / law enforcement / defence, not de-identified statistical research integration), and uses DIFFERENT tools (Foundry/Gotham, not Splink). No documented overlap of body, dataset, or tool. Note one ostensible adjacency: Services Australia (Medicare) and DSS (Centrelink) feed the ABS Spine, and these are mainstream service-delivery agencies — but Palantir has no documented contract with either for the Spine or any other purpose.
profile.contract_values_dates: Defence: ~$26m+ cumulative since 2013; $7.6m one-year contract via AusTender 11 Feb 2026 (limited tender), Cyber & Electronic Warfare Division; earlier 2024 contract ~$7.1-7.15m (Foundry). AUSTRAC: $8.1m in 2023, grown past $12m via five variations within ~12 months. ACIC: ~$5.7m across contracts/extensions (hardware/software maintenance 2015-16 to 2023-24, shifting to data analytics from 2024-25). Total federal spend on Palantir reported ~$50m (since 2016-17) to ~$60m, with ~$26m of that since 2023-24. Future Fund equity stake reported >$160m. (Figures sourced from journalism citing AusTender; treat precise totals as approximate.)
profile.operational_use: Defence: Foundry-based data integration/analytics to map and manage defence-industry capacity and supply-chain logistics for the Cyber & Electronic Warfare Division; reporting indicates embedded Palantir staff. AUSTRAC: data analytics over financial-transaction/AML intelligence. ACIC: integrating and searching criminal-intelligence data — linking people, events and locations; the documented system held ~42m data points (intercepted calls/texts, police/government databases, phone/address location data) with workflows for warrant-number automation and geographic mapping. Splink/ABS side is operationally separate: probabilistic linkage of administrative datasets to a Spine for de-identified statistical/research use, not operational policing or targeting.

Germany — Bundesverfassungsgericht (Federal Constitutional Court), Judgment of 16 February 2023 (1 BvR 1547/19 and 1 BvR 2634/20): provisions of the Hesse Security and Public Order Act and the Hamburg police data-processing act authorising automated data analysis were unconstitutional, violating the right to informational self-determination for lacking sufficient interference thresholds. The software at issue was Palantir's 'hessenDATA' (Gotham-based), used by Hesse police since 2017; Hamburg's provision was void, Hesse's allowed to continue under restrictions until new law by 30 Sept 2023. Brought by Gesellschaft fuer Freiheitsrechte (GFF). This is the landmark adjudication touching Palantir, but it is a German precedent — not binding in Australia and not connected to Australia's Splink/ABS use.

profile.country_or_body: Canada (federal DND/CANSOFCOM + Ontario Provincial Police); Splink side = Ontario MCCSS and Environment and Climate Change Canada
profile.summary: Palantir has a substantial, well-documented Canadian government footprint, but it sits in DIFFERENT bodies and a DIFFERENT data domain than Canada's documented Splink use. Federally, the Department of National Defence (DND) ran Palantir Gotham for the Canadian Special Operations Forces Command (CANSOFCOM/JTF2) under a sole-source contract that grew from CAD 14.4M (2020) to ~CAD 44.4M via 12 amendments (DND reports ~CAD 46.8M spent), preceded by a CAD 997,434 sole-source pilot (March 2019), plus a separate ~CAD 3.7M Foundry evaluation (used intermittently 2022-2024). Provincially, the Ontario Provincial Police (OPP) confirmed using Gotham for crime analysis (2022) and was re-awarded a CAD 36.6M contract in October 2023. Canada's documented Splink use, by contrast, is self-reported by the Ontario Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services (MCCSS) Data Integration Unit and by Environment and Climate Change Canada — social-services and environmental administrative data linkage, NOT policing or defence. There is NO documented Palantir presence in MCCSS or Environment Canada, and no evidence Palantir uses or touches Splink. The strongest geographic overlap is the province of Ontario (Palantir in OPP / Splink in MCCSS), but these are different agencies, different tools, and different data domains, so the honest tier is SAME_DOMAIN_SAME_COUNTRY (same country/province, different body and domain), edging toward THEMATIC_ADJACENCY because the data domains differ (policing vs social assistance/environment). NOTE: Statistics Canada uses its own G-Link tool (and the Social Data Linkage Environment/SDLE), not Splink.
palantir_presence: DND / CANSOFCOM (JTF2): Palantir Gotham, sole-source, CAD ~14.4M (signed 27 Mar 2020) expanded to ~CAD 44.4M via 12 amendments through ~Mar 2025; DND reports ~CAD 46.8M total spent. Disclosed only in Sept 2025 after Conservative MP questioning; withheld for ~5 years on national-security grounds. Integrates/collates data from disparate stores for analyst visualization with access controls.
DND / CANSOFCOM: CAD 997,434 one-year sole-source evaluation licence for an information-processing tool, signed 28 Mar 2019 (the first publicly disclosed federal Palantir procurement).

DND: Palantir Foundry, ~CAD 3.7M, used intermittently May 2022-Mar 2024 as an evaluation activity for data processing/analytics; DND says no longer active. A 2025-reported CAD 3.7M figure was the basis for press concern about military data access.

Ontario Provincial Police (OPP): Palantir Gotham for crime analysis, confirmed Oct 2022; CAD 36.6M contract re-awarded Oct 2023 after a Sept 2022 Request for Qualifications (9 vendors qualified). Builds a searchable knowledge graph fusing crime/arrest records, ALPR hits, social-network mappings, physical descriptions, tattoos, vehicle info, relational links. Configuration undisclosed, citing investigative techniques.

Federal Software Licensing Supply Arrangement (SLSA): Palantir added Jan 2024-Jul 2028 via Carahsoft as distributor, making products available to all federal departments/agencies/Crown corps through simplified procurement (CanadaBuys ProServices/TBIPS/SLSA records exist for Palantir Technologies Canada Inc.).
profile.palantir_product: Palantir Gotham (DND/CANSOFCOM and OPP); Palantir Foundry (DND evaluation 2022-2024)
profile.operational_use: Defence/special-forces intelligence fusion and analyst visualization (Gotham for CANSOFCOM/JTF2); provincial policing crime analysis via a fused knowledge graph (OPP Gotham ingesting crime reports, arrest records, ALPR/license-plate hits, social-network mappings, descriptions, vehicles, relational links). Foundry was a federal data-processing/analytics evaluation. Palantir does NOT perform record-linkage of social-assistance or environmental data — that is the Splink domain (MCCSS, Environment Canada).
profile.contract_values_dates: CAD 997,434 (28 Mar 2019, sole-source pilot, CANSOFCOM); CAD ~14.4M signed 27 Mar 2020 expanding to ~CAD 44.4M (12 amendments through ~Mar 2025), DND reports ~CAD 46.8M spent (Gotham, CANSOFCOM); ~CAD 3.7M Foundry (used May 2022-Mar 2024); OPP CAD 36.6M (awarded Oct 2023, use confirmed Oct 2022); SLSA supply arrangement 23 Jan 2024-4 Jul 2028.
profile.splink_side: Canada's documented Splink users are the Ontario MCCSS Data Integration Unit (Splink is its main tool for intra-/inter-ministerial social-services data linking; MCCSS Social Assistance database 2003-2016 linked to health data such as the Registered Persons Database) and Environment and Climate Change Canada (linking administrative/reporting program datasets). Both are self-reported (e.g., via the UK MoJ Splink project's user list and CRDCN/PopData webinars). Palantir relates to this only by SHARED COUNTRY and, for MCCSS, SHARED PROVINCE (Ontario) — but a DIFFERENT body (OPP, not MCCSS; DND, not Environment Canada), a DIFFERENT tool (Gotham/Foundry, not Splink), and a DIFFERENT data domain (defence/policing vs social assistance/environment). No overlap of dataset, agency, or tool; Palantir does not use Splink.

OPP Gotham: crime reports, arrest records, ALPR/license-plate-reader hits, social-network mappings, physical descriptions/tattoos, vehicle information, relational links

adjudications: Canada — Conflict of Interest and Ethics Commissioner (Mario Dion), 16 Sept 2020: ruled Palantir Canada president David MacNaughton contravened the Conflict of Interest Act by offering Palantir's services to the federal COVID-19 response; nine officials (incl. Freeland, Bains) ordered to restrict dealings with him for one year. MacNaughton consented and acknowledged the breach. (No contract resulted.) Also a related Office of the Commissioner of Lobbying matter.

Germany — Federal Constitutional Court (BVerfG), Judgment of 16 Feb 2023, 1 BvR 1547/19 & 1 BvR 2634/20: held Hesse and Hamburg automated-data-analysis policing provisions (implemented via Palantir Gotham, 'hessenDATA') unconstitutional for violating informational self-determination; Hamburg act void, Hesse provision time-limited to 30 Sept 2023. profile.country_or_body: EU institutions — Europol, Frontex, European Medicines Agency (EMA)
profile.summary: The Splink "users" page lists the European Medicines Agency (EMA) as using Splink to detect duplicate adverse-event reports for veterinary medicines (EudraVigilance Veterinary) — a pharmacovigilance/data-quality use, NOT a Palantir product. Palantir has NO documented presence at EMA. Palantir's actual EU-institution footprint is at EUROPOL, where its Gotham platform was procured via a Capgemini framework contract (signed 2012, ~EUR 7.5m for a new Europol Analysis System), entered operational big-data use from ~2016, and was used until Q3 2021 before Europol replaced it. Europol's Palantir dealings are extremely opaque (released only 2 of 69 requested documents, citing trade secrets / public security; one disclosed record showed Europol even considered legal action against Palantir). FRONTEX: no Palantir contract found — Frontex's analytics rely on Microsoft, not Palantir. Because the Splink entry (EMA, veterinary pharmacovigilance) and Palantir's documented EU footprint (Europol, law enforcement / counter-terror data mining) sit in entirely different bodies AND different data domains, the connection is THEMATIC_ADJACENCY at most: both reflect EU bodies "joining up" previously siloed records, but there is NO same-body, same-domain, or same-dataset overlap. Do not claim Palantir uses Splink — there is no such link. The landmark adjudication touching Palantir is Germany's Federal Constitutional Court (BVerfG) judgment of 16 Feb 2023, which struck down Hesse/Hamburg laws authorising automated data analysis (Hesse ran Palantir Gotham as "hessenDATA") as unconstitutional — but that is a member-state matter, not an EU-institution one.
palantir_presence: EUROPOL — Palantir Gotham: procured via French consultancy Capgemini under a framework contract signed in 2012 (reported value ~EUR 7.5m, 'new Europol Analysis System'); Gotham reportedly entered operational use ~2016 for big-data evaluation and ran until the third quarter of 2021, when Europol said it terminated/replaced the licence. The framework contract related to customised Palantir software is reported to have expired Dec 2019.

EUROPOL — what it did with citizen data: Gotham ingested and cross-linked massive personal datasets (phone/communications records, social-media posts, images, profile metadata) to identify patterns and map networks of individuals; deployed against Europol's large analytical data stores.

FRONTEX — NO documented Palantir contract found. Frontex's data-analytics stack is reported to rely on Microsoft BI, not Palantir. (Advocacy campaigns lump Frontex with Palantir/ICE rhetorically, but no Frontex–Palantir procurement is evidenced.)
EMA — NO Palantir presence found. EMA is the Splink user (veterinary adverse-event deduplication in EudraVigilance Veterinary); no Palantir Foundry/Gotham contract with EMA was identified.
profile.splink_side: The Splink documentation's users/use-cases page states verbatim: "The European Medicines Agency uses Splink to detect duplicate adverse event reports for veterinary medicines." This is the EU entry on the Splink page. Splink is the UK MoJ-built open-source probabilistic record-linkage (Fellegi-Sunter) Python library; EMA applies it to deduplicate suspected-adverse-event reports in its EudraVigilance Veterinary (Union Pharmacovigilance Database) — a data-quality/pharmacovigilance function. Palantir relates to this ONLY thematically: it operates in a DIFFERENT EU body (Europol) and a DIFFERENT data domain (law-enforcement/counter-terror network analysis), with no shared body, dataset, or tool. There is no evidence Palantir uses, supplies, or touches Splink, and none that Palantir is involved in EMA pharmacovigilance.
profile.palantir_product: Palantir Gotham (at Europol, as the Europol Analysis System; in Germany/Hesse as 'hessenDATA'). Foundry is Palantir's health/life-sciences platform but no EMA deployment was found.
profile.contract_values_dates: Europol/Palantir Gotham via Capgemini framework contract signed 2012, reported ~EUR 7.5m; operational use ~2016–Q3 2021; framework contract for customised Palantir software reportedly expired Dec 2019. No Frontex or EMA Palantir contract values exist (none found).
profile.operational_use: Europol used Gotham for automated big-data analysis: linking communications records, social media, images and profile metadata to map networks of individuals and surface patterns across Europol's analytical data holdings. EMA's (non-Palantir) Splink use is operational duplicate-detection within veterinary pharmacovigilance reporting.
datasets: Europol analytical data stores / Europol Analysis System (incl. the bulk 'data lake' the EDPS scrutinised — reported by The Guardian at ~4 petabytes)
Communications/phone records, social-media posts, images, profile metadata processed via Gotham at Europol
Germany/Hesse hessenDATA linked POLAS, ComVor, CRIME-ST police databases plus traffic and telecommunications data (Palantir Gotham)
EMA EudraVigilance Veterinary / Union Pharmacovigilance Database — veterinary adverse-event reports (Splink, NOT Palantir)
adjudications: Germany — Federal Constitutional Court (BVerfG), Judgment of 16 Feb 2023, 1 BvR 1547/19 & 1 BvR 2634/20: struck down §25a Hesse Security and Public Order Act (HSOG) and §49 Hamburg Police Data Processing Act (HmbPolDVG) authorising automated data analysis/'data mining' for crime prevention. Hesse (which ran Palantir Gotham as hessenDATA) deemed incompatible but allowed under strict conditions until 30 Sept 2023; the Hamburg provision was voided entirely. Court held automated analysis is a distinct interference with informational self-determination (Art. 2(1) + Art. 1(1) Basic Law) requiring separate, strict justification. NB: member-state ruling, not an EU-institution ruling, and does not name Palantir in the operative text but concerns Palantir's software.

EU — EDPS (European Data Protection Supervisor): not a court ruling but a regulator decision. 3/10 Jan 2022 order requiring Europol to erase data on individuals with no established link to criminal activity (Data Subject Categorisation), imposing a 6-month retention/filtering window; concluded a 2019 inquiry; concerned Europol's ~4PB bulk data store. This regulator action targets Europol's data-handling generally; sources do NOT establish it was specifically about the Palantir Gotham system. EDPS also separately objected to Europol using Clearview AI (a company linked to Palantir co-founder Peter Thiel).
controversies: Extreme opacity: Europol released only 2 of 69 documents on its Palantir dealings, invoking trade secrets / public security; Dutch authorities reportedly hold 45,000+ Palantir-related documents.
Privacy advocates (Statewatch, EDRi, MEPs) called for a European probe into Palantir; parliamentary questions filed (E-003872/2020, E-000951/2022).
Reported that Europol considered legal action against Palantir; Gotham reportedly proved 'substandard' and incompatible with Europol's IT environment.
Peter Thiel / Clearview AI linkage flagged by EDPS as likely violating the Europol Regulation.
German BVerfG ruling became a landmark constraint on Palantir-style dragnet/automated police data analysis in the EU.

verification.notes: Connection tier THEMATIC_ADJACENCY is correctly assigned and survives adversarial review — there is NO same-body, same-domain, or same-dataset overlap, and crucially NO documented technical link between Splink (UK MoJ open-source record-linkage used by EMA for veterinary pharmacovigilance) and Palantir (Europol law-enforcement Gotham). title: UN / humanitarian — Palantir + WFP, UNHCR, refugee data

profile.country_or_body: UN / Humanitarian sector — World Food Programme (WFP) [Palantir] vs. UNHCR [Splink/fastLink]
profile.summary: Palantir's documented humanitarian footprint is with the UN World Food Programme (WFP), NOT with UNHCR. In Feb 2019 WFP announced a 5-year, reported-$45m partnership using Palantir Foundry to unify WFP's operational, supply-chain and beneficiary data across ~80-120 countries; it drew a major civil-society backlash (65-signatory open letter via the Responsible Data community). On the Splink side, it is UNHCR (a DIFFERENT UN body) that uses probabilistic record-linkage to deduplicate refugee registration lists — and crucially, UNHCR's public code (unhcr-americas/record_linkage) actually uses fastLink, while Splink only LISTS UNHCR as a user. So Palantir and the Splink/record-linkage use sit in the SAME humanitarian data domain but in DIFFERENT bodies (WFP vs UNHCR) and via DIFFERENT, unrelated tools (Foundry vs Splink/fastLink). There is no documented Palantir contract inside UNHCR, and Palantir does not use Splink. The connection is thematic: both reflect the trajectory of aggregating/linking vulnerable-population (refugee/displaced) data. The lead's framing of the "$45m Building Blocks deal" conflates two separate WFP projects: the Palantir analytics deal AND "Building Blocks" (WFP's permissioned-blockchain cash-transfer project in Jordan that integrates UNHCR iris-biometric IDs) are distinct — Building Blocks is not a Palantir product.
palantir_presence: WFP–Palantir partnership, announced 5 Feb 2019: 5-year deal, reported value ~US$45 million (value not confirmed in WFP's own press release; the $45m figure originates in press/Privacy International reporting). Palantir Foundry brings together WFP data sources (programmatic, operational, supply-chain, beneficiary/partner/supplier/transporter data) into a 'secure, unified environment' for HQ and field staff.
Pre-2019 pilot: Palantir's 'Optimus' tool used for food procurement/supply-chain optimization in Iraq; WFP cited >US$26-30m in savings, with claimed potential up to ~US$100m/year at scale.

Partnership EXPANDED/continued through 2020-2026; Privacy International (Jun 2026) describes Palantir as 'the cornerstone of the WFP data ecosystem,' now spanning ~120 countries, with partner data potentially integrated into Foundry without informed consent.

NO documented Palantir contract with UNHCR, UN OCHA, or WHO found in this research. References to Palantir + COVID-19 relate to WFP collaboration framing but no separate UNHCR/WHO contract was verified.
profile.splink_side: UNHCR appears on Splink's user list and performs probabilistic record linkage / deduplication of refugee registration lists (e.g., merging lists from field partners to build survey sampling universes, Unique Entity Estimation). However, UNHCR's actual public tutorial/code (github.com/unhcr-americas/record_linkage) implements fastLink (R), not Splink, and references a DANE/Colombia UN Statistics Commission example. Palantir relates to this ONLY by domain adjacency: Palantir's Foundry sits in a DIFFERENT UN body (WFP), does data INTEGRATION/analytics rather than statistical record-linkage, and there is no evidence Palantir touches UNHCR's registration data or uses Splink. Same humanitarian data domain, different body, different (unrelated) tool.
profile.contract_values_dates: Announced 5 Feb 2019; 5-year term. Reported value ~US$45 million (per press / Privacy International; NOT stated in WFP's own release — flag as unconfirmed in primary source). Pre-deal Optimus pilot claimed US$26-30m savings; partnership extended/expanded through to at least 2026.
profile.operational_use: Within WFP: data unification and analytics via Foundry — operational planning, supply-chain/logistics optimization, food-procurement cost reduction, and management of programmatic + beneficiary/partner/supplier/transporter data across ~80-120 countries serving ~90 million people. WFP states no personally identifiable information is shared with Palantir and that all data remains under WFP control; critics dispute this is meaningful given mosaic-effect/re-identification risk.
datasets: WFP operational, supply-chain and procurement data in Palantir Foundry
WFP data on partners/suppliers/transporters/beneficiaries integrated into Foundry
UNHCR refugee registration lists deduplicated via fastLink/Splink (separate from Palantir)
WFP 'Building Blocks' blockchain + UNHCR iris-biometric IDs (separate from Palantir)
controversies: 65-signatory open letter (Feb 2019) organized by the Responsible Data community (incl. Privacy International) urging WFP to release the agreement terms, procurement/due-diligence info, establish an independent review panel, and ensure privacy/security.

Concerns over re-identification / 'mosaic effect': WFP's 'no PII shared' assurance challenged as largely invalid because aggregated humanitarian data can re-identify individuals and reveal population-movement patterns.
Aggregating refugee/displaced-population data in a CIA-In-Q-Tel-funded US national-security firm; described by critics as 'breathtaking and terrifying' and 'horribly irresponsible.'

title: Gambia / Laos / low-income adopters — Palantir presence (likely negative)

profile.country_or_body: The Gambia and Lao PDR (Laos) — low-income Splink adopters (Gambia census deduplication; Lao PDR Shared Child Health Record pediatric record linkage)
profile.summary: No evidence of any Palantir Technologies presence — contract, deployment, pilot, partnership, or proposal — in either the Gambia or Laos was found across web search, Palantir's SEC disclosures, serious journalism, and the company's own customer disclosures. Palantir's documented government footprint is concentrated in the United States, the Five Eyes (UK), Western/Central Europe (Germany, France, Netherlands, Denmark, Norway), Ukraine, Israel, and select Asia-Pacific allies (notably Japan, South Korea, and Singapore). Mainland low-income Southeast Asia (Laos, Cambodia, Myanmar) is served, where at all, by Chinese surveillance technology transfers, not Palantir. Sub-Saharan low-income Africa, including the Gambia, does not appear in any Palantir customer list. The honest, expected result holds: NONE_FOUND for both countries. This clean negative is meaningful: it shows the two poorest Splink adopters use a free, open-source MoJ tool for benign statistical/health record-linkage with zero Palantir entanglement, sharpening the contrast with rich-country jurisdictions (e.g., Germany's Hesse) where Palantir Gotham was deployed for police data analysis and later constrained by the Federal Constitutional Court. There is NO claim — and no evidence — that Palantir uses or touches Splink anywhere; the two are unrelated tools and the Gambia/Laos link to Palantir is simply absent.

None found in Laos (Lao PDR): no procurement record, SEC-disclosed contract, news report, or Palantir customer listing references any Lao government body. Surveillance buildout in mainland SE Asia (Myanmar PSMS, Cambodia) is documented as Chinese-technology-driven, not Palantir.

Closest geographic/thematic adjacencies (still NOT Gambia/Laos and NOT government-data deployments there): Palantir's humanitarian work with the UN World Food Programme (a 2019 data partnership, widely criticised by ~60 civil-society groups) operates across many countries but is not documented as a Gambia or Laos government deployment of citizen data; and Palantir's COVID-era public-health tooling was UK/US-centric. Neither establishes any Gambia or Laos footprint.
profile.palantir_product: Not applicable — no Palantir product (Gotham, Foundry, AIP, Apollo) is deployed in the Gambia or Laos. (For context, the product adjudicated in Germany was Palantir Gotham, branded 'HessenDATA' in Hesse.)

For contrast only: where Palantir does operate on government data (e.g., Hesse police 'HessenDATA' / Gotham since 2017), it ingests and cross-links pre-existing police databases into a single searchable graph for crime prevention/investigation — a fundamentally different activity from the statistical/health record deduplication Splink performs in the Gambia and Laos.

adjudications: Germany — Federal Constitutional Court (Bundesverfassungsgericht), Judgment of 16 February 2023 (1 BvR 1547/19, 1 BvR 2634/20): held § 25a(1) Hessian Security and Public Order Act (HSOG) and § 49(1) Hamburg Police Data Processing Act unconstitutional for violating the right to informational self-determination; the automated data-analysis powers lacked sufficient thresholds (no requirement of an identifiable danger). The Gambia — none found (no court or regulator ruling touching Palantir).
Laos — none found (no court or regulator ruling touching Palantir).
profile.splink_side: Splink is the UK Ministry of Justice's free, open-source probabilistic record-linkage / entity-resolution library (moj-analytical-services/splink). The prior global analysis flags two low-income adopters: the Gambia (using Splink for census deduplication) and Lao PDR (using Splink to de-duplicate pediatric records in a Shared Child Health Record project, notable for non-Latin-script matching). Both are benign statistical/health-administration uses. Palantir relates to this in NO way: it does not provide, use, integrate with, or compete for these Splink deployments; there is no shared body, no shared dataset, and no Palantir product in either country. The relationship is therefore strictly NONE_FOUND — the two countries sit on the open-source/public-good end of the record-linkage spectrum, the polar opposite of the proprietary Palantir Gotham police deployments adjudicated in Germany. No claim is made that Palantir uses Splink.

title: Palantir’s global government strategy & which Splink-countries it has penetrated

profile.country_or_body: GLOBAL CROSS-COUNTRY SCORECARD: Palantir's government footprint across the 10 documented Splink-adopter jurisdictions (UK, Australia, US, Chile, Germany, Canada, Gambia, Laos, EU, UNHCR)
profile.summary:
This is the comparative backbone synthesising (a) Palantir's global government strategy and financials and (b) which Splink-adopter countries have a documented Palantir government footprint, with per-country tiering. CRITICAL FRAMING: Splink and Palantir are NOT the same thing and there is NO evidence Palantir uses Splink. Splink is the UK MoJ's free, open-source probabilistic record-linkage Python library used by statistics/research bodies to JOIN UP citizen records (e.g. census deduplication). Palantir (Gotham/Foundry/Maven) is a proprietary, paid data-integration and analytics platform used operationally by police, immigration, defence and intelligence bodies. Where both appear in the same country they are almost always (i) different government bodies, (ii) different tools, and (iii) different purposes (statistical research vs. operational enforcement/targeting). The honest connection is THEMATIC/DOMAIN-LEVEL ("the quiet joining-up of citizen records by government"), NOT a contractual or technical link.

FINANCIALS (Palantir SEC filings / Q4 2025): FY2025 revenue ~$4.5bn; ~54% government / ~46% commercial; full-year government revenue +53% YoY, commercial +60% YoY; US commercial accelerating to triple-digit YoY growth by Q3 2025. Government remains the larger and strategically anchoring segment.

GLOBAL STRATEGY: (1) US defence anchor — Maven Smart System DoD IDIQ raised from $480m (May 2024) to a ~$1.3bn ceiling through 2029; ~$10bn US Army enterprise agreement (July 2025). (2) US immigration — ICE 'ImmigrationOS' $30m sole-source (Apr 2025), up to $159m, through Sep 2027; fuses IRS/SSA/DMV/passport/ALPR data; civil-liberties groups warn it captures data on naturalised citizens and legal residents by design. (3) NATO — Maven Smart System NATO contract via NCIA signed 25 Mar 2025 for Allied Command Operations (one of the Alliance's fastest-ever procurements). (4) Ukraine — Maven battle-tested (mixed results); Jan 2026 Brave1 Dataroom built on Palantir infrastructure. (5) Israel/IDF — Jan 2024 'strategic partnership' / 'Partnership for Battle Tech' with Israel's MoD; serious-journalism and NGO allegations (Al Jazeera, BHRRC, BDS) that Palantir tech supports Gaza targeting/'kill list' workflows (UNVERIFIED in specifics; Palantir disputes characterisations; tools 'Lavender'/'Gospel' are IDF-attributed, not proven Palantir products). (6) Five Eyes / Europe — embedded across UK (NHS FDP, ~34+ state contracts, £670m+ cumulative per Lighthouse Reports), Australia, Canada, and EU policing (Europol Gotham).

TIERING RESULT across the 10 Splink jurisdictions: UK = SAME_DOMAIN_SAME_COUNTRY (borderline DIRECT — NHS England is BOTH a Splink user and the Palantir FDP customer, but different tools/functions); Germany = SAME_DOMAIN_SAME_COUNTRY (Destatis uses Splink; police forces use Palantir; + the landmark BVerfG adjudication); US, Australia, Canada = SAME_DOMAIN_SAME_COUNTRY (Palantir present in-country but in different bodies than the Splink adopters); EU = SAME_DOMAIN_SAME_COUNTRY (Palantir at Europol vs. Splink at EMA — different EU bodies); Chile, Gambia, Laos = NONE_FOUND; UNHCR = THEMATIC_ADJACENCY (no Palantir-UNHCR contract found, but Palantir has humanitarian-data partnerships, e.g. WFP).
palantir_presence: UK / NHS England: Federated Data Platform (FDP) — Palantir Foundry-based — awarded Nov 2023 to Palantir-led consortium (with Accenture, PwC, NECS, Carnall Farrar). Published award ~£182.2m, max envelope up to £330m to 2030, 3+2+1+1 structure (first term ends Mar 2027); first-year spend >=£25.6m. Operational, direct-care data integration across trusts. Lighthouse Reports: 34+ UK state contracts across 10+ departments, £670m+ cumulative.

Germany / Police (Hesse, Hamburg, Bavaria): Palantir Gotham — Hesse ran it as 'hessenDATA' from 2017; Hamburg adopted a parallel legal basis; Bavaria runs it as 'VeRA' (cross-case research/analysis platform linking millions of records incl. witnesses, victims, informants). Operational policing data analysis / association-mapping.

US / ICE: 'ImmigrationOS' $30m sole-source (Apr 2025), up to $159m, through Sep 2027 — fuses IRS, SSA, DMV, passport, license-plate-reader data to identify/apprehend removal targets and track self-deportations; flagged for sweeping in naturalised citizens/legal residents.
US / DoD: Maven Smart System — IDIQ raised from $480m (2024) to ~$1.3bn ceiling through 2029; plus ~$10bn US Army enterprise deal (July 2025). AI-enabled intelligence fusion and targeting.

Australia / Defence & agencies: $7.6m Defence 'Industrial Intelligence Capability' contract (Foundry, limited tender, 2026) mapping defence-industry supply chains; plus ~$25m+ cumulative since 2013 across ASD, ACIC, AUSTRAC (>$12m after variations), NSW Crime Commission, Victorian Dept of Justice.

Canada / National Defence: $46.8m contract (awarded 27 Mar 2020, expanded via 12 amendments without retender) for Canadian Special Operations Forces Command (incl. JTF2); separate ~$14m DND data-management contract; Jan 2024 53-month Supply Arrangement opening Palantir licences to federal departments.
EU / Europol: Palantir Gotham embedded in Europol core operations (route via 2012 Capgemini contract); Palantir also embedded in multiple EU member-state police forces.
Ukraine: Maven battle-tested; Brave1 Dataroom (Jan 2026) on Palantir infrastructure. NATO: Maven Smart System NATO via NCIA (Mar 2025). Israel: MoD 'strategic partnership' (Jan 2024).
profile.splink_side: Splink (UK MoJ open-source probabilistic record-linkage library) adopter mapping per the maintainer-curated 'Use Cases' page: UK = pervasive (ONS, NHS England, MoJ Data First, MoD, UKHSA, DfE, CMA, Homes England, Welsh Revenue Authority, ~10 local councils); Australia = ABS (statistics); US = Defense Health Agency (200M+ records), Florida Cancer Registry; Germany = Destatis (register-based census linkage); Canada = Ontario Min. of Children/Community/Social Services (main tool), Environment & Climate Change Canada; Chile = Min. Health + UCL (migrant immunisation linkage); Gambia = 2024 census/post-enumeration linkage; Laos = child health record deduplication; EU = European Medicines Agency (EMA); UNHCR = refugee dataset deduplication. HOW PALANTIR RELATES: In EVERY case the Splink user is a STATISTICS / HEALTH-RESEARCH / CENSUS / SOCIAL-SERVICES / HUMANITARIAN body using a free open-source tool for de-duplication/linkage, whereas Palantir's documented footprint is in POLICING / IMMIGRATION / DEFENCE / INTELLIGENCE bodies using a proprietary operational platform. Same country and same broad data-domain (joining-up citizen records) in UK, Germany, US, Australia, Canada, EU — but DIFFERENT body + DIFFERENT tool + DIFFERENT purpose. Strongest overlap is UK NHS England (a single body that is both a Splink user for research linkage AND the Palantir FDP customer for operational/direct-care integration — still different tools, different functions). No body anywhere is documented using Splink and Palantir for the same dataset/task; Palantir does NOT use Splink.
profile.palantir_product: Gotham (police/intelligence: Germany hessenDATA/VeRA, EU Europol); Foundry (data integration: UK NHS FDP, Australia Defence IIC); Maven Smart System (DoD/NATO/Ukraine AI targeting & intelligence fusion); ImmigrationOS (ICE deportation platform, Foundry-based); AIP (Artificial Intelligence Platform, cross-segment)
profile.operational_use: See structured field; Palantir's documented citizen-data use across these jurisdictions is operational enforcement/targeting/direct-care integration, categorically distinct from the Splink adopters' statistical/research record-linkage. The two streams represent the same societal trajectory ('quiet joining-up of citizen records') executed by different arms of the state with different tools.
profile.contract_values_dates: UK NHS FDP: Nov 2023, ~£182.2m award / up to £330m to 2030 (first term ends Mar 2027). US ICE ImmigrationOS: Apr 2025, $30m sole-source / up to $159m through Sep 2027. US DoD Maven: $480m (May 2024) raised to ~$1.3bn ceiling through 2029; ~$10bn US Army deal (July 2025). NATO Maven (NCIA): 25 Mar 2025. Israel MoD strategic partnership: Jan 2024 (reported multi-hundred-million). Australia Defence IIC: $7.6m (2026, limited tender); ~$25m+ cumulative since 2013. Canada DND/CANSOFCOM: $46.8m (awarded 27 Mar 2020, 12 amendments); ~$14m DND data-mgmt; Supply Arrangement Jan 2024–Jul 2028. Palantir FY2025: ~$4.5bn revenue, ~54% government.
adjudications: Germany — Bundesverfassungsgericht (Federal Constitutional Court), Judgment of 16 February 2023, 1 BvR 1547/19 and 1 BvR 2634/20 (brought by Gesellschaft für Freiheitsrechte / GFF). Held that the statutory bases for automated police data analysis in Hesse (§25a HSOG) and Hamburg (§49 Hamburg police data law) violated the right to informational self-determination. Hamburg's provision declared VOID; Hesse's incompatible with the Basic Law but allowed conditionally until 30 Sep 2023, restricted to particularly serious offences with specific facts indicating danger to life/limb or state security. Palantir IS named: the Court noted Hesse 'purchased the Gotham operating system from the software company Palantir and ran it under the name hessenDATA' (2017). The Court found such dragnet 'analysis by association' a distinct, serious constitutional interference.
Germany — follow-on constitutional complaint filed by GFF on 23 July 2025 against Bavaria's use of Palantir ('VeRA'); pending before the BVerfG (no ruling yet as of June 2026).

No court/regulator adjudication found specifically condemning the UK NHS FDP, US ICE, Australian, or Canadian Palantir contracts as of June 2026 (political/parliamentary and FOI scrutiny exists, but not a binding judgment).
controversies: Israel/Gaza: Jan 2024 IDF/MoD strategic partnership; Al Jazeera, Business & Human Rights Resource Centre and BDS allege Palantir tech supports Gaza targeting / 'kill lists' (UNVERIFIED specifics; IDF tools 'Lavender'/'Gospel' are not proven to be Palantir products; Palantir disputes the characterisation).
US ICE ImmigrationOS: ACLU, EFF, American Immigration Council and members of Congress (Menendez letter) warn it tracks Americans/naturalised citizens and enables mass-deportation 'mission creep'.
Germany: GFF litigation; civil-rights objection to opaque 'black-box' US software creating long-term police dependency.

UK NHS FDP: medConfidential/Medact/openDemocracy campaigns over patient-data trust, heavily-redacted published contract, low trust uptake, procurement concerns; Peter Thiel's prior disparaging NHS comments.
Procurement transparency: Australia (limited/no-tender), Canada (secret $46.8m contract, 12 amendments, 'not for public disclosure'), EU Europol (opaque tech relationships per Statewatch).
Founder/ownership: Peter Thiel association drives much of the political controversy.
datasets: UK NHS FDP: trust-level operational health/care records (de-identified when shared nationally)
Germany hessenDATA/VeRA: police-held records incl. suspects, witnesses, victims, informants
US ImmigrationOS: IRS tax, SSA, DMV, passport, license-plate-reader, immigration-status data
US DoD/NATO Maven: ISR / sensor / intelligence feeds (military, not citizen civil records)
Australia: defence-industry supply-chain data (IIC); AUSTRAC financial-intelligence; criminal-intelligence (ACIC)
Canada: special-forces/defence operational data; possible OSINT/social-media analysis (per reporting)
EU Europol: cross-border law-enforcement / criminal-intelligence data
unverified_flags: Israel/Gaza targeting: claims that Palantir specifically powers IDF 'kill lists' / 'Lavender' / 'Gospel' are allegations from journalism/NGOs, NOT court-confirmed; Lavender/Gospel are IDF-attributed systems not proven to be Palantir products. Do not state as fact.
Israel MoD contract dollar value ('multi-hundred-million') is reported, not from a verified primary contract document.
Canada facial-recognition / civilian-social-media analysis: asserted by some outlets (drugdatadecoded.ca) as a capability, not confirmed operational use against Canadians.
UNHCR–Palantir: NO contract found; Palantir's documented humanitarian link is with the World Food Programme (WFP), a different organisation — treat UNHCR as thematic adjacency only.
Chile, Gambia, Laos: no Palantir government footprint found in this research pass (search-summary negatives, not exhaustively proven absent).
Lighthouse Reports £670m / 34-contract UK figures are reputable-journalism aggregates; individual line items not each independently re-verified here.

profile.caveats: Confidence HIGH on the structural finding (Palantir and Splink are different tools used by different bodies for different purposes; no Palantir-uses-Splink link; tiering as above) and on the primary-sourced items (BVerfG judgment, SEC financials, NHS FDP, ICE/Maven contracts, Splink adopter list). Confidence is LOWER on: (1) Israel/Gaza targeting specifics (allegation-level, disputed); (2) exact Israel MoD contract value; (3) absence of Palantir in Chile/Gambia/Laos/EMA/UNHCR (these are 'not found in this pass', i.e. negative evidence, not proof of absence). The single most defensible cross-country pivot for the post is GERMANY: Destatis (Splink, statistics) vs. Hesse/Hamburg/Bavaria police (Palantir Gotham/VeRA, operational) + the BVerfG Feb 2023 ruling — same country, same data-joining domain, different body/tool/purpose, with a binding constitutional adjudication naming Palantir. The UK is the tightest single-body overlap (NHS England uses both, different functions). Do NOT assert any contractual or technical relationship between Palantir and Splink anywhere.
